# Agent Best-Practices Benchmark — how the GPU desk stacks up against documented practice

**Date:** 2026-07-03
**Provenance:** Generated by the `deep-research` harness (fan-out web search → fetch → 3-vote
adversarial verification → synthesis). 5 search angles, 21 sources fetched, 105 claims extracted,
**25 verified (19 confirmed, 6 refuted, 0 unverified)**. Confidence labels and refuted-claim
warnings below are from that verification pass — treat them as load-bearing, not decoration.
**Scope:** benchmarks this system's design against best practice, sorts the pain points into
*normal* vs *self-inflicted*, and recommends a prioritized toolset. Companion to the
gathering-aim diagnosis (see "Companion diagnosis" at the end).

---

## TL;DR (the verdict)

1. **The architecture is a named, canonical pattern — not improvised.** "Deterministic code gates +
   LLM reasoning + enforced citations," orchestrator dispatching tool-less worker subagents, and a
   post-hoc citation pass are all documented best practice. Staying on Claude Code skills instead of
   a framework (LangGraph/Temporal/Agent SDK) is *explicitly endorsed*.
2. **Nearly every pain point is a documented, expected failure class of agentic systems — not bad
   harnessing.** Roughly 70% "normal maturation," ~20% data economics (paywalls), ~10% genuine
   plumbing gaps.
3. **The one clearly self-inflicted gap is the missing eval harness** — and best practice says it's
   cheap to close (~20 queries + rubric LLM-as-judge, not a golden set of hundreds). Highest-leverage
   next move.
4. **Concrete tools:** build the ~20-query eval now · borrow Graphiti's bi-temporal + canonical-ID
   *architecture* for the memory fixes · add SEC primary sourcing (free EDGAR, or sec-api.io $49/mo) ·
   harden gates to multi-level (evidence-sufficiency, not just schema) · Brier-score the theses.
5. **Honest gap:** the search-API/scraper tooling comparison (Tavily/Exa/Brave/Firecrawl/…) produced
   **zero surviving verified claims** — it needs a hands-on follow-up pass, and it's the very thing
   the leading-headline problem depends on.

---

## 1. Architecture — you're already on the documented pattern

| Finding | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Anthropic's taxonomy formally defines "workflows" with literal **"gates"** (programmatic checks on intermediate steps) and the **orchestrator-workers** pattern (a lead LLM delegating to workers + synthesizing). This *is* the desk's design. | high (3-0) | anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents |
| A dedicated **post-hoc CitationAgent** that attaches every claim to a source location *after* generation (vs trusting inline cites) is the shipped production pattern in Anthropic's Research system. | high (3-0) | anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system |
| Orchestrator + parallel subagents **outperformed single-agent by 90.2%** on Anthropic's internal research eval. | high (3-0) *with caveats* | (same) |
| Staying with **simple, composable orchestration** instead of frameworks is what the most successful production agents did; add complexity "only when needed." | high (3-0) | anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents |

**Caveats to carry:** the 90.2% figure is an *internal, unpublished-methodology* eval, used ~15×
more tokens than single-agent (not cost-normalized), and ran on now-superseded models (Opus 4 /
Sonnet 4); never independently replicated. It's scoped to breadth-first, parallelizable research —
which the gathering workload fits — and explicitly *excludes* shared-context/interdependent tasks.

**Refinement this suggests:** our citation gating trusts the extraction/judgment brains' inline
`findingIds`. The production pattern runs a *separate* citation-verification stage over the finished
output + sources. Worth considering a dedicated citation-audit pass rather than only gating ids at
write time.

---

## 2. Which pain points are normal vs self-inflicted

**Normal (documented, expected — do not read as our failure):**

- SOTA open-source multi-agent systems fail **41–86.7%** of the time (MAST, UC Berkeley, **NeurIPS
  2025**, 1,642 annotated traces across 7 frameworks). *high (3-0).*
- A separate 15-mode taxonomy names **run-to-run inconsistency on deterministic prompts**,
  multi-step reasoning drift, incorrect tool invocation, and version drift as **system** failures,
  not model failures. *high (3-0).* → our judgment whipsaw + schema drift + re-dispatch loops.
- Non-accumulating memory is a known decomposable class (summarize / store / retrieve). The
  **NVDA-vs-nvidia collision is a storage-stage reconciliation failure**; standard fix =
  **canonical entity IDs at write time + an explicit consolidation step**. *medium.* → our F24/F52/F53.

**Self-inflicted (fixable, on us):**

- **No eval harness.** This is the gap. See §4.
- The gathering *aim* problem (indicator design + weak query seeds + budget spent on price pages) —
  see companion diagnosis. Self-inflicted and separate from the paywall question.

**Data economics (neither harness nor process — a sourcing choice):** paywalled TrendForce/
SemiAnalysis and the thin primary base. License, build cheap primary pipelines (EDGAR), or keep the
honest `estimate` label. Charter Part 22 already commits to "inventoried, never scraped."

---

## 3. Gates must be multi-level (the whipsaw handle)

> MAST Insight 3 (verbatim, high / 3-0): "the presence of a verifier is not a silver bullet… More
> rigorous verification is needed, such as using external knowledge, collecting testing output
> throughout generation, and **multi-level checks for both low-level correctness and high-level
> objectives**." (Adding high-level objective verification gave +15.6% on ChatDev.)

**Application:** our gate today is largely final-stage + schema/registry validity. Add a **high-level
objective gate** — e.g. *"is there enough fresh, corroborated evidence to justify **changing** the
binding constraint this cycle?"* — i.e. anti-whipsaw for dimension ratings, in deterministic code.
This is the named best-practice backing for the dimension-rating stickiness already flagged.

**Keep aggregation/freshness in code, not the brain.** LLM freshness judgment fails via
**prior-override** (model emits its training prior despite an explicit "newer wins" rule) and
**serial-comparison drift** (loses track across many conflicting candidates). *medium (2-1); mechanism
stands, effect sizes were refuted.* Reinforces the "LLM extracts, code decides" split for indices/deltas.

---

## 4. The eval harness — the one gap, and it's cheap

Best practice does **not** require a large golden dataset. *high (3-0), anthropic.com Research system:*

- Start with **~20 queries** representing real usage.
- Grade with a **rubric LLM-as-judge** on five criteria: **factual accuracy, citation accuracy,
  completeness, source quality, tool efficiency** — plus **human spot-review**.
- Why small works: early effect sizes are large (a prompt tweak can move success **30%→80%**), so a
  handful of cases already catches regressions. "Start small-scale right away" beats waiting for scale.
- Corroborated by Anthropic's "Demystifying evals" (Jan 2026: 20–50 tasks from real failures) and
  LangChain's ~20-labeled-examples checklist.

This maps to F6's second half (rubric-graded golden set + prompt-regression gate). Do it **first** —
it de-risks every prompt change the other fixes require.

---

## 5. Calibration — score the theses

*high (3-0), Forecasting Research Institute (ForecastBench operators):* the right yardstick for
forecast-like outputs (theses, binding-constraint calls) is **Brier score against resolution over
time** — the superforecasting track-record discipline.

- Oct 2025: best LLM **0.101** Brier vs human superforecasters **0.081** (LLMs trail ~25%).
- Jan 2026 update: superforecasters still lead (gap 0.017; best LLM Grok 4.20 Preview 0.102);
  projected parity **~Nov 2026** (95% CI Jan 2026 – Nov 2027).

**Takeaway:** log every thesis as a probabilistic call and Brier-score it as triggers resolve. Do
not assume the judgment is calibrated until the track record says so. Note: the "LLMs trail
superforecasters" framing may expire within months — re-check before quoting.

---

## 6. Concrete toolset (with pricing, verified 2026-07-03)

| Need | Tool | Pricing | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary sources** | **SEC EDGAR (free API)** | $0 | Zero-cost, authoritative. Full-text search covers 2001+, structured Form 4 2003+. |
| Primary sources (ergonomic) | **sec-api.io** | first 100 calls free; **$49/mo** annual ($55 monthly); 50 GB/mo | Full corpus ~20M filings, 500+ form types, 1993–present, 1.1M+ entities. 10-K/10-Q/8-K/13F/Form 4. |
| **Memory / entity ID** | **Graphiti** (open source, powers Zep) | $0 (OSS) | Bi-temporal model (4 timestamps: `t_valid`/`t_invalid` vs `t'_created`/`t'_expired`) → stable fact identity + day-over-day deltas. Entity resolution = 1024-dim embedding + full-text + LLM merge → NVDA-vs-nvidia. **Adopt for architecture, NOT benchmarks (perf claims refuted).** Test on financial aliasing before committing. |
| Earnings transcripts | Finnhub | **Premium-gated — NOT free** | `/stock/transcripts*` endpoints require paid Premium; a paid *Basic* subscriber is still blocked. Do not treat as a free pipeline. |

---

## 7. ⚠️ Refuted claims — do NOT act on these

Verification killed these; they were plausible but did not survive. Recorded so future-me doesn't
resurrect them:

- **MAST's per-category failure percentages** (43.8% / 31.85% / 24.5%) — refuted (1-2). Cite the
  41–86.7% *range* and the taxonomy's *existence*, not category frequencies.
- **Zep/Graphiti performance wins** — DMR 94.8% vs MemGPT 93.4% (0-3) and LongMemEval +18.5% /
  −90% latency (1-2) both refuted. Graphiti stands on *architecture*, not benchmarks.
- **Deterministic-aggregation +10.8pp** effect and "bottleneck = post-retrieval aggregation" —
  refuted. The *mechanism* (prior-override, serial-drift) stands; the *effect sizes* do not.
- **Finnhub archive depth** (220k recordings / 7TB / 15yr / 5 countries) — refuted (1-2). Don't cite
  Finnhub coverage figures.

**Source-quality caveats:** architecture-validation findings rest on Anthropic's own blog posts
(authoritative for what Anthropic built/recommends, but vendor-authored). Three findings rest on
non-peer-reviewed preprints (MemFail May 2026; deterministic-freshness May 2026 at 2-1; the 15-mode
taxonomy Nov 2025, single-author). Only MAST is peer-reviewed. Pricing/gating is time-sensitive.

---

## 8. Open questions (unresolved by this pass)

1. **Which search/scraper stack** (Tavily vs Exa vs Brave vs SerpAPI; Firecrawl vs Jina vs crawl4ai
   vs Playwright) actually wins for a ~20-docs/cycle market-intel workload on Windows, and what are
   legitimate, terms-compliant paths to JS-rendered / bot-protected pages? **No claims survived
   verification** — needs hands-on benchmarking or a targeted follow-up research pass. *This is the
   piece the leading-headline problem depends on.*
2. What do TrendForce/SemiAnalysis and transcript providers actually cost at solo scale; is there a
   licensing-clean transcript source cheaper than Finnhub Premium?
3. At what concrete complexity threshold does graduating to the Agent SDK / LangGraph / Temporal pay
   off? Anthropic's "stay simple until needed" gives no quantified trigger.
4. Does Graphiti's heuristic entity resolution hold up on financial-domain aliasing (tickers vs
   legal names vs product lines) at our scale? Its benchmark claims were refuted; run a small
   domain-specific canonicalization eval before committing.

---

## 9. Recommended action order

1. **Build the ~20-query rubric eval** (§4) — the self-inflicted gap; cheapest high-leverage win;
   de-risks every prompt change below. (F6 second half.)
2. **Harden gates to multi-level** (§3) — evidence-sufficiency check to suppress binding-constraint
   whipsaw. Pure code, no deps.
3. **Add SEC primary sourcing** (§6) — free EDGAR first; fix the primary/secondary tier classifier
   (momentum is mislabeled 0/N primary today).
4. **Borrow Graphiti's bi-temporal + canonical-ID design** for the memory/series-identity fixes
   (F24/F52/F53) — architecture, not benchmarks.
5. **Brier-score the thesis book** (§5).
6. **Targeted follow-up research pass on the gathering stack** (open question 1) — the RQ3 gap; needs
   hands-on benchmarking, not another literature sweep.

---

## Companion diagnosis — the gathering *aim* problem (separate from this research)

This research validated a **primary-source depth** fix (SEC/EDGAR) but did **not** solve the
*between-earnings leading-signal* gap, which is self-inflicted and complementary. From direct
inspection of the 2026-07 / 07-02 / 07-03 cycles:

- The **scoring spine is wired to quarterly filings** — ~11 of 17 indicators are `cadence: quarterly`;
  every score-moving fundamental (D2, grossMargin, S9, S10, market-share-pct, rpoBacklog,
  vendorRevenueGuidance) is earnings-sourced, and the gather attempts filing URLs *first*. The monthly
  had 25/72 findings from May earnings; 31/72 were ≥60 days old.
- The **daily budget is spent on the wrong thing** — dailies are fresh (median 1 day) but ~half the
  findings are `gpuSpotPrice`/`D6` price-page scrapes, which are **overlay-only (F8) and never feed
  DMI/SMI**. Both dailies produced **0 outlook/leading findings**.
- The **headline sources are starved** — `open-web-asic`, `channel-checks`, `open-web-gpu-share`,
  `vendor-pr-trade-press` return `not-covered`/paywalled every cycle. Only `designWins` (weekly) and
  `leadTimes` (weekly) can turn a fresh headline into a score-moving finding; most news has nowhere
  to land.

**Fixes (no frozen-core change):** add a between-earnings leading-indicator layer · sharpen free-web
seeds into recency-filtered headline queries (+ RSS/news angles) · cap price fetches at 2–3 and
redirect budget to leading-news leads · stop re-fetching filings mid-quarter · raise/priority-order
`maxDocuments`.

---
